summarization.a: Stacked XRD patterns comparing TiN and Ti–Si–N films deposited by plasma-enhanced ALD. The TiN film exhibits clear polycrystalline TiN reflections with a dominant (200) orientation, whereas the Ti–Si–N film shows strongly suppressed and broadened TiN-related features, indicating a largely X-ray amorphous or nanocrystalline structure due to silicon incorporation.

data_extraction.a:
| Sample Name | Labeled Peak | 2θ (°) | Observed Intensity & Shape |
|-------------|--------------|--------|----------------------------|
| TiN PEALD   | TiN(111)     | ~36–37 | Low to moderate, sharp peak |
|             | TiN(200)     | ~42–43 | High, sharp peak (dominant orientation) |
|             | Si substrate | ~55     | Very high, sharp peak      |
|             | TiN(220)     | ~61–62  | Low, sharp peak            |
| Ti–Si–N PEALD | TiN-related features | ~36–43 | Very weak, broad features |
|               | Si substrate | ~55        | Very high, sharp peak      |

a.question: What is the crystalline phase of the pure TiN PEALD film?
a.answer: Polycrystalline rock-salt TiN with strong (111), (200), and (220) peaks.

a.question: Which TiN diffraction peak is most intense in the TiN PEALD spectrum?
How does the XRD response of the Ti–Si–N film differ from that of the TiN film?
a.answer: The Ti–Si–N film shows strongly weakened and broadened TiN-related features compared with the sharp, well-defined reflections in TiN. This indicates a substantial reduction in long-range crystalline order caused by silicon incorporation.

a.question: Does the Ti–Si–N film exhibit clear long-range crystallinity detectable by XRD?
